intro: Z A U N   B I O M E D I C A L
Containment Facility - NV-1
Six months ago, Zaun Biomedical announced a breakthrough.
NV-1 - a neuro-regenerative compound capable of repairing
catastrophic spinal damage. Human trials began immediately.
Three weeks ago, all contact with the facility went dark.
No distress signal. No evacuation report. No survivors.
The official story: a containment failure. An accident.
You were part of the research team.
You know it wasn't an accident.
You wake up on an examination table with no memory of
the last 72 hours. A nurse named Emily is standing over you.
The facility is overrun. The exits are sealed.
And somewhere in the north wing, Dr. Zaun is still here -
and he hasn't tried to leave.
Type 'look' to examine your surroundings.
Type 'look at <item>' to inspect something.
Type 'take <item>' to pick something up.
Type 'go <direction>' to change rooms.
Type 'quit' to exit.

rooms.description:
The EXAM ROOM:
Cold fluorescent lights hum softly above. The examination table where you woke up still stands at the center of the room, its restraints hanging loose at the sides.
Medical instruments are scattered across a nearby tray - some clean, others... not. A faint metallic smell lingers in the air.
The walls are sterile, almost identical from every angle, making the room feel smaller than it should be. A monitor beside the table flickers intermittently, displaying unreadable data.
You've been here before. Not long ago.

npcs.name: emily
npcs.description:
A nurse in bloodstained scrubs.
She looks like she hasn't slept in days, and still she's here.

dialogues.text:
Emily grips your arm before you can stand.
'Don't move too fast. You were in bad shape when I found you.' She glances toward the door.
'The generator is dead - no power means no security systems, no locked doors, and no way to access the research terminal in the east wing. We need fuel. I saw a canister in the research lab before things went wrong.'
She pauses, voice dropping.
'And if you run into Dr. Zaun - don't engage. Don't listen to what he says. Just walk away.'

rooms.description:
THE CENTRAL LOBBY:
looks like the moment of collapse was captured and preserved.
Chairs are overturned mid-flight. A security desk has been torn from its bolts. Dried blood traces long drag-marks across the tile floor, leading nowhere.
The monitors above the reception counter are shattered, their frames still warm.
To the north, a reinforced security door - thick steel, no visible handle, a red indicator light blinking slowly above it.
Whatever is behind that door, someone wanted it to stay there.

enemies.name: standard infected
enemies.description:
What used to be a security guard moves through the lobby with a lurching, broken rhythm.
One leg drags uselessly. Its mouth opens and closes around the same syllables, over and over - the remnants of a sentence it no longer understands.
This is early-stage NV-1: the higher brain functions are gone, but the body hasn't caught up yet.
It is slow. It is weak. And it will not stop until you do.

rooms.description:
THE RESEARCH LAB:
has been ransacked, but not by the infected - the destruction here is too deliberate.
Filing cabinets have been emptied and upended. Terminal screens are cracked but not smashed. Papers litter the floor in organized piles, as if someone was sorting through them when they had to stop.

enemies.name: clicker
enemies.description:
A former researcher, now unrecognizable.
Advanced NV-1 infection causes catastrophic neural pressure buildup - eventually the optic nerves fail completely and the eyes fuse shut. What survives develops something else instead: an acute sensitivity to sound and light that borders on sonar.
The soft clicking sound it makes is how it maps the room.
Move quietly.
Or don't move at all.

interactables.name: terminal
interactables.description:
A security terminal bolted to the eastern wall.
The screen is cracked but intact. A faded Zaun Biomedical logo peels from the casing.
Under normal circumstances this would control access to the entire north wing - but right now the screen is black and cold.
interactables.successMessage:
The terminal shudders to life as the power comes back online.
The Zaun Biomedical logo flashes once, then dissolves into a single prompt:
> NORTH WING ACCESS - CLEARANCE CODE REQUIRED
The cursor blinks, waiting.
interactables.failureMessage:
The terminal is completely dead.
Without power to the facility, it's just a cracked screen and a faded logo.

npcs.name: emily
npcs.description: Emily moves carefully around the room, checking the floor before each step.

dialogues.text:
Emily keeps her voice barely above a whisper.
'The terminal is live. Zaun used a custom access code for the north wing - he didn't trust the standard facility protocols.'
She hesitates.
'I found fragments of it in the research records. Two numbers on separate pages. He split it deliberately. Read everything carefully'

Emily stares at the terminal screen without blinking.
When she finally speaks, her voice is hollow.
'NV-1 wasn't a failed experiment. It was never meant to heal anyone. Zaun built it to do exactly this - and then he stayed behind to watch.'
She looks at you.
'The door is open. He's waiting for you up there. Be careful. He wants you to walk through that door.'

rooms.description:
THE GENERATOR ROOM:
smells of diesel, burnt wiring, and something organic you don't want to identify.
Emergency strip lighting along the floor casts everything in dim red.
The generator itself - a massive diesel unit the size of a car - sits silent in the center of the room, fuel gauge needle resting on empty.

enemies.name: ripper
enemies.description:
NV-1 doesn't always destroy - sometimes it rebuilds in the wrong direction entirely.
In a small percentage of subjects, the compound triggered extreme musculoskeletal restructuring. The spine shortened. The arms lengthened.
The result moves on all fours with terrifying speed and hunts by scent.
This one circles the generator in slow, deliberate loops, claws leaving deep gouges in the concrete.
It knows you're here.
It's deciding when.

interactables.name: generator
interactables.description:
A Harmon Industrial backup generator - designed to keep the facility running for 72 hours in a blackout.
Someone drained it dry. A fuel intake port sits on the side, cap missing.
interactables.successMessage:
You twist the intake cap free and pour the fuel canister into the generator.
The machine coughs, kicks, and then catches - a deep mechanical growl that shakes the walls.
Overhead, the fluorescent lights stutter and flare to life.
Somewhere in the facility, something hums back to power.
interactables.failureMessage:
The generator won't start.
The fuel gauge reads completely empty - you need to find fuel first.

rooms.description:
THE CONTAINMENT LABORATORY:
Everything here is deliberately ordered - which makes it worse. The bodies are arranged along the walls. The containment chambers are ranked by size, smallest to largest.
And in the center of the room, bathed in the amber glow of a single massive sealed tank, stands Dr. Zaun.
He is not panicked. He is not running.
He is watching the door as if he has been waiting for exactly this moment, for exactly this long.

npcs.name: zaun
npcs.description:
Dr. Arlen Zaun.
Lead researcher on the NV-1 project. The man who started all of this - and stayed to watch it finish.

dialogues.text:
Zaun clasps his hands behind his back, the way a professor would at the front of a lecture hall.
'You made it further than any of the others. I had hoped you would.'
He glances at the containment chambers.
'You think NV-1 was an accident. A failure. I understand why - that is what the records were designed to suggest.'
A faint smile.
'This facility was never a hospital. It was a proof of concept. And you are standing in the results.'

Zaun hasn't moved from his position near the tank.
He watches the chaos with the calm of someone observing a controlled experiment - which, you realize, is exactly what this is to him.

Zaun surveys the stillness of the room. The bodies of his subjects. The shattered chambers.
He looks almost satisfied.
'Remarkable. Every single one.'
He turns to face you fully for the first time.
'I did not build a fail-safe into this facility. I built a selection mechanism. You are what survives.'
He spreads his hands.
'So. What do you do with that?'

rooms.description:
THE EXIT ROOM:
A narrow emergency corridor, stripped of everything non-essential.
Bare concrete walls. A single overhead light.
At the far end, a heavy reinforced fire escape hatch sits sealed with a magnetic lock - the kind that needs a high-clearance keycard to release.
Through the gaps in the frame you can feel it: cold air. Real air. The city is on the other side of that door.
Whatever is left of it.

interactables.name: keycard
interactables.description:
A reinforced emergency exit hatch.
The magnetic lock requires a high-clearance keycard. A small reader blinks red beside the handle.
interactables.successMessage:
You press Zaun's keycard against the reader.
A beat of silence - then a heavy magnetic clunk as the lock releases. The hatch swings outward on its own weight.
Cold night air floods the corridor, carrying the smell of smoke and rain and open sky.
Emily steps through first. You follow. Behind you, the hatch seals shut.
ENDING: SURVIVAL
interactables.failureMessage:
The reader blinks red.
The lock holds.
You need a high-clearance keycard to open this.

items.name: notebook
items.description:
Emily pressed this into your hands before you were fully awake.
Her handwriting - hurried but precise, the handwriting of someone who has been keeping notes under pressure for a long time.
The cover reads:
'READ THIS FIRST. ALL OF IT.'
---
INFECTED (Stage 1 - the slow ones)
They still look human. They're not. The higher brain is gone but the body keeps moving. They're weak and they're slow - a sharp edge is enough. 
The broken bottle will do it. 'STAB' them before they get close enough to matter.
---
CLICKERS (Stage 2 - the blind ones)
Don't let the stillness fool you. They map everything by sound. Their eyes are fused shut - NV-1 destroyed the optic nerves and sealed them over. 
That's the weakness: they can't handle sudden light. 'FLASH' them with the flashlight and overload them completely. Get it out before they hear you move.
---
RIPPERS (Stage 3 - the fast ones)
These don't walk anymore. The mutation restructured the skeleton - they move on all fours and they are fast. Don't try to outrun one.
The scrap metal is heavy enough to drop them if you commit to the swing. One solid 'STRIKE' to the skull.
---
BRUTES (Stage 3 - the large ones)
I don't know how to explain this gently so I won't. A bottle won't penetrate. The scrap metal alone won't reach deep enough. 
But I watched one go down when someone 'COMBINE'd the two - glass edge bound to the metal, something with reach and a real cutting edge. 
Make the blade first. Then 'STAB' it.
---
A final line at the bottom of the page, underlined:
'I wrote this so you don't have to figure it out the hard way -> 73xx

items.name: flashlight
items.description:
A heavy tactical flashlight - the kind issued to facility security.
The casing is cracked along one side but the beam still works, cutting hard and bright.
Someone has taped a handwritten warning to the grip:
'STAGE 2 SUBJECTS - BLIND VARIANTS - EXTREME LIGHT SENSITIVITY. DO NOT APPROACH WITHOUT ILLUMINATION. DIRECT EXPOSURE TRIGGERS ACUTE NEURAL OVERLOAD. SUSTAINED BEAM = TERMINATION.'
Below it, in different handwriting, someone added:
'It works. I tested it.'

items.name: bandages
items.description:
A sealed roll of sterile bandages from the exam room supply cart.
Someone kept this cart fully stocked long after the facility protocols collapsed - Emily, probably.
The packaging is torn at one corner. She may have already used some.
If something gets close enough to open you up, this is the difference between slowing down and stopping entirely.

items.name: research notes
items.description:
Typed pages, but with heavy handwritten annotations - two different people arguing in the margins.
The typed text describes NV-1 as a neuro-regenerative compound designed to repair spinal damage. Several subject entries are marked STAGE TRANSITION and then FAILED:
'Stage 2: Optic nerve failure confirmed in 100% of surviving subjects. Neural pressure reroutes through auditory cortex. Subjects develop echolocation-adjacent spatial awareness. 
Extreme photosensitivity. Approach only with sustained light source.'
'Stage 3-A: Skeletal restructuring in 8% of Stage 2 survivors. Quadrupedal adaptation. Massively increased muscle density. Blunt trauma ineffective past a certain threshold - recommend reinforced impact tools.'
One section is circled in red ink, the annotation reading 'YOU NEED THIS':
'Glass-edge weapons reinforced with rigid metal demonstrate significantly improved penetration against Stage 3-A musculature.'
At the very bottom, half a code scrawled in red: 14.

items.name: fuel canister
items.description:
A yellow emergency diesel canister, still sealed, stenciled with the Zaun Biomedical facility crest.
Heavy enough that carrying it takes both hands.
It should be more than enough to restart the backup generator - assuming the generator itself is still intact.

items.name: scrap metal
items.description:
A heavy strip of structural steel, probably torn from a shelf bracket or door frame.
Jagged on one end. The other end has been wrapped in torn fabric - improvised grip.
The dents along the flat edge are deep and irregular. This has been used before, against something that hit back. The dark staining isn't rust.
On its own it's a bludgeon.
But the research notes mentioned something about reinforced glass edges. Combined with the right material, this could become something more deliberate.

items.name: broken bottle
items.description:
A thick laboratory reagent bottle, shattered cleanly at the neck.
The remaining glass is dense and sharp - not elegant, but the edge holds. You've used worse.
It won't last long and it won't reach deep on its own.
But the notes were specific about glass edges reinforced with metal. If you had something rigid to bind it to, you could make this into a real weapon.

items.name: keycard
items.description:
A matte black security keycard.
No name - just a clearance level printed in small text: OMEGA-7. Zaun's level. The highest in the facility.
The embedded chip pulses faintly when you hold it under light, still drawing power from somewhere.
Whatever locks are still tied to this facility's magnetic system, this will open them.
There is only one door left that requires it.

zaun_phases.message:
Zaun moves to the first containment column and enters a code without looking at the keypad.
He has done this before.
The chamber seals disengage with a pressurized hiss and three figures stumble forward - slow, broken, still wearing what they arrived in. Stage 1. Early infection.
He watches them the way you'd watch a timer run down.

Zaun turns to the next column without a word.
These chambers are darker.
The moment the seals release, the sound begins - a rapid, rhythmic clicking that fills the room like static. Two figures emerge.
Their faces are wrong: eye sockets sealed shut beneath fused scar tissue, heads tilting in sharp precise angles as they map the room by sound alone.
Stage 2.
He has been keeping them fed.

The final chamber is the largest.
The door is reinforced with additional bolts that Zaun unlocks one by one, methodically, like he is unwrapping something precious.
When it finally opens, the sound precedes the thing itself - a low, grinding drag of something massive moving on all fours.
It fills the doorframe.
What NV-1 does to most subjects in six days, it has done to this one completely. The bones restructured. The muscle density is extraordinary.
It does not hesitate.
